steps.explanation: When you multiply by 10, each digit shifts one place to the left. A zero appears in the ones place.
When you multiply by 100, each digit shifts two places to the left. Two zeros appear.
When you multiply by 1,000, each digit shifts three places. Three zeros appear.

steps.work: 6 → 60 → 600 → 6,000. Each step adds one more zero.
steps.explanation: The number of zeros in 10, 100, or 1,000 tells you how many zeros to add to the product.
The same pattern works for multi-digit numbers.
Every digit shifts one place left. The 2 moves to hundreds, the 3 to tens, and 0 fills ones.
Multiplying by 10 always shifts digits one place to the left, no matter how many digits the number has.
